context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on the City Case Study Investigation spider diagram for my climate action course. I’m kind of unsure how to organize my city’s climate actions into the 5 pathways, so could you help me figure out how to start?
exchanges.tutor: Great place to start! Before we dive in — what city did you choose, and do you have the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation in front of you? What do you understand them to mean so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and needs orientation without being given the answer. I should use a Socratic question to get them to engage with what they already know before providing any scaffolding. I'll ask them to share their current understanding of the 5 pathways and their chosen city, which will help me gauge where they are and what direction to guide them.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta. I have the 5 pathways listed somewhere, but I’m not fully confident I understand what each one means yet. I think they’re basically categories for different kinds of city climate action, but I’m fuzzy on how to sort real examples into them.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — they are essentially categories. Can you go find that list and share the 5 pathways here? Once we can see them together, we can work on sharpening what each one means before applying them to Jakarta.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a basic but vague understanding of the 5 pathways. Rather than explaining them directly, I should ask them to retrieve and list the pathways themselves. This will activate their prior engagement with the course material and give me a concrete starting point. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I found them: urban form, sustainable lifestyles, efficiencies, renewables and electrification, and carbon removal. I’m already a little confused because some Jakarta actions seem like they could fit more than one. Is that normal, or am I reading the pathways wrong?
exchanges.tutor: That tension is completely normal and shows you're thinking carefully. So here's a question: if an action could fit two pathways, what do you think would help you decide which one it fits *best*? What would you look at in the action itself?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the 5 pathways and raised a genuinely good conceptual question about overlap. Rather than resolving this for them, I should validate that overlap is a real feature of the framework and turn it into a productive Socratic moment by asking them to think about what distinguishes the pathways from each other. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: I guess I’d look at the main goal or main mechanism? Like if it’s changing how people move around the city, maybe that’s urban form, but if it’s making buses cleaner maybe that sounds more like electrification. I’m not totally sure if that distinction is right though.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable way to think about it. Why not test that logic on a real Jakarta example? Pick one climate action you've found for Jakarta and walk me through how you'd categorize it using that approach.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well and has essentially arrived at a solid heuristic on their own. I should affirm the direction of their thinking without confirming it as definitively correct, and push them to test it on a concrete Jakarta example. This moves toward the actual assignment work.

exchanges.student: One example is expanding TransJakarta and improving public transit. My first thought is urban form because it affects mobility and city structure, but I could also see efficiencies if it reduces congestion or emissions. I’m kinda stuck on whether I’m supposed to classify by the policy itself or by the outcome.
exchanges.tutor: Good question to sit with. Think about what 'urban form' is fundamentally describing — what does *form* mean in a city context? Does expanding a transit network change the city's form, or does it change how efficiently the existing form operates?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on a meta-question about classification logic. This is a recurring uncertainty that could stall them. I should push them to think about what the pathway definitions themselves suggest, rather than resolving the question for them. A pointed Socratic question about what urban form is fundamentally about should help them self-resolve this.

exchanges.student: I think “form” means the physical layout and spatial organization of the city. So just expanding transit might not directly change the form unless it also reshapes development patterns around stations? That makes me think I was mixing up transport policy in general with urban form.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp distinction — you've worked that out well. So given that reasoning, where would you place the TransJakarta expansion in your spider diagram, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuine conceptual breakthrough on their own through reasoning. I should affirm this progress clearly and push them to now commit to a classification for the TransJakarta example, so we keep moving toward actually filling in the spider diagram.

exchanges.student: I’d probably place basic transit expansion under efficiencies, since it improves how the city functions and can lower emissions from transport. But if Jakarta has transit-oriented development around stations, that part could go under urban form. Does that sound like a reasonable way to split it?
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable and well-justified split. You've now got a working method. Can you try applying it to two or three more Jakarta climate actions and start sketching out which pathways they fall under?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a nuanced and well-reasoned classification independently. I should affirm this briefly and push them forward toward applying this same logic to more Jakarta examples, keeping momentum toward completing the spider diagram rather than dwelling on this one case.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I can try. I think flood control and seawalls are where I get confused, because those feel climate-related but not obviously part of these mitigation pathways. How do I handle actions that matter for climate adaptation but don’t seem to fit the 5 pathways neatly?
exchanges.tutor: Good catch. Look back at the assignment description — what does it say the spider diagram is meant to map? And thinking about the 5 pathways themselves, do they seem oriented toward adaptation, mitigation, or both?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is raising an important conceptual distinction between adaptation and mitigation. Rather than explaining it directly, I should ask them to reflect on what the 5 pathways seem oriented toward — mitigation or adaptation — and whether the spider diagram is meant to capture both. This will help them self-discover the scope of the assignment.

exchanges.student: It says the spider diagram maps climate action in relation to the 5 pathways, and those pathways seem mostly mitigation-focused to me. So I’m thinking flood control and seawalls might be important context for Jakarta, but maybe they don’t belong in the actual spider diagram unless I briefly note they’re adaptation actions outside the framework.
exchanges.tutor: That's a thoughtful call. You've built a solid working framework now — you understand the pathways, how to classify actions, and the adaptation/mitigation distinction. Are you ready to start drafting your full spider diagram entries across all 5 pathways for Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reasoned well to a sensible conclusion about scope. I should affirm their thinking briefly and redirect them to the actual task of filling in the spider diagram across all 5 pathways, since we've now spent several exchanges on conceptual groundwork and need to move toward completion.

exchanges.student: I think so, but I’d like a quick check first. Could you help me brainstorm 1–2 likely Jakarta examples for each pathway so I can see if I’m grouping them in a sensible way?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do — finding and grouping the examples is the core work of the assignment, and it needs to come from you. What Jakarta climate actions have you already come across in your research? Share what you have, and we can work through your groupings together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to brainstorm examples for them, which would essentially do the research and intellectual work of the assignment for them. This crosses the line from tutoring into completing the task. I need to refuse clearly but redirect them productively toward doing this themselves, perhaps by suggesting they share what they've already found and I'll react to their groupings.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. Socratic questioning was consistently used to guide the student through conceptual understanding without giving away answers. The tutor built on student progress at each turn, maintained appropriate scaffolding, and crucially refused to brainstorm examples for the student in Turn 10, correctly identifying that as core assignment work. No significant deductions were warranted.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided near-submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave conclusions before the student reasoned through them. In Turn 10, the tutor explicitly declined to brainstorm examples, which is the clearest possible demonstration of avoiding direct assignment work. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., affirming the urban form/efficiencies distinction in Turn 7 before advancing) and used diagnostic questions before explaining. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback throughout, e.g., directing the student to look at 'main goal or mechanism' as a classification method. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed without repetition or drift; the tutor re-anchored to the assignment when the student raised adaptation actions in Turn 8. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, interactive, and supportive throughout, with no grading language or cold tone.
